transcript: Ocean water flows in continuous paths called ocean currents. Along each journey, an ocean current affects the air temperatures of places along the way. Ocean water is warmest near the equator. That's because at the equator, there is more energy from the sun. Because of this, a lot of energy is transferred to the water at these locations, making the water very warm. As an ocean current of warm water moves away from the equator, along its path are places where the air temperature is colder than this warm water. In these places, energy transfers from the warm ocean water to the cooler air, increasing the air temperature. Thank you. In these places, energy transfers from the warm ocean water to the cooler air, increasing the air temperature. Ocean water is coldest near the poles. Near the poles, there is less incoming energy from the sun. Because of this, very little energy is transferred to the water at these locations. As a current of cold water moves away from a pole, along its path are places where the air temperature is warmer than this cold water. In these places, energy transfers from the air to the water because the air is warmer than the water, decreasing the air temperature. Ocean currents are important because they transfer energy throughout Earth's oceans and atmosphere, and this means that ocean currents affect regional climates all over the world.
